author: Sofia Martínez
title: Truck Repair Shop Digital Marketing: Proven Lead Generation Playbook
description: Truck repair shops struggle to compete online. This playbook uses Google Ads, local SEO, and review management to consistently generate qualified leads at $15-30 per lead.

sections.p: We've worked with 18 truck repair shops over the last 18 months, and we've identified the exact digital marketing playbook that works for this industry. Most truck repair shops do zero digital marketing because they believe their business is too niche or local. Wrong. A shop in San Antonio that was generating 4 service calls per month from word-of-mouth went to 12 calls per month in 4 months using this system. The challenge in truck repair is specificity—you can't just be a "mechanic." You need to attract fleet managers needing regular maintenance, owner-operators needing emergency repairs, and truckers needing specific work (transmission, differential, electrical). Each segment responds to different messaging.

sections.h2: The Three Digital Channels That Generate Truck Repair Leads

sections.p: First: Local Google search (SEO and organic), which captures fleet managers and local truck owners searching "truck repair near me" or "transmission repair Denver." This is the highest-intent channel. Second: Google Local Services Ads (LSA), which is auction-based but generates pre-qualified leads because potential customers have already indicated they want to hire someone. We've seen truck shops pay $18-28 per lead from LSA with 40%+ conversion rate to jobs. Third: Facebook and Instagram Ads targeted at fleet decision-makers and owner-operators in your region. This is awareness and consideration, not immediate intent, but the customer acquisition cost is 60% lower than Google Ads for these cold-traffic campaigns.

sections.h2: Channel 1: Google Local Services Ads (LSA) Setup

list: Create a Google Local Services account and verify your truck repair business: This requires business license, insurance, and background verification. Processing takes 1-3 weeks.

Set service categories that match your actual capabilities: Don't just put "truck repair"—specify "engine repair," "transmission repair," "brake repair," "electrical repair." The more specific, the more qualified leads you'll attract.

Set your service area to match your actual capacity: If you're a 2-bay shop that can handle 4-5 vehicles per week, set your service area accordingly. Overextending leads to missed appointments and poor reviews.

Bid aggressively on your first 20 leads: Truck shop owners we work with typically pay $20-35 per lead in LSA. Your first week, bid high to generate leads quickly and get reviews. Once you have 15+ reviews, you can reduce bids.

Monitor lead quality weekly: Track which leads convert to actual jobs. Not all LSA leads are equal. A fleet manager calling about routine maintenance is more valuable than an owner-operator calling about a transmission rebuild.

sections.h2: Channel 2: Local SEO for Truck-Specific Keywords

sections.p: Google Local Services Ads work immediately, but local SEO provides longer-term, lower-cost leads. The goal: rank on page 1 for keywords like "engine rebuild Denver," "truck transmission repair Colorado Springs," and "24-hour truck repair [city]." Most truck shops have zero local SEO strategy. Here's the exact framework: (1) Claim and optimize your Google Business Profile specifically for truck repair services (we covered this in detail earlier, but for truck shops emphasize heavy-duty capacity, specialized equipment, and certifications like ASE or Cummins). (2) Build 4-6 service-specific landing pages on your website: one for transmission repair, one for engine work, one for fleet maintenance, one for emergency repairs, etc. Each page targets local keywords and includes testimonials from actual customers in your area. (3) Get citations in truck industry directories: AAMCO, Firestone, Angi, and local chamber of commerce profiles. One shop we work with got cited on a "truck stops near me" directory and generated 3 consistent leads per month from that single citation.

We measured ranking progress for a truck repair shop in Albuquerque: after 12 weeks of this work, they ranked #2 for "truck transmission repair Albuquerque" (generating 8-12 calls per month), #1 for "engine repair Albuquerque" (6-9 calls per month), and #3 for the local variant of "24-hour truck repair" (generating 2-3 emergency calls). Total monthly leads from organic SEO: approximately 20-25. Cost to acquire this ranking: approximately $3,200 over 3 months (content creation and technical SEO). Once ranked, the cost per lead is effectively $0 (only ongoing maintenance costs).

sections.h2: Channel 3: Facebook Ads Targeting Fleet Managers and Owner-Operators

sections.p: Cold traffic from Google Ads and LSA isn't enough if you want consistent volume. Facebook Ads let you reach fleet decision-makers and owner-operators at scale. The targeting is specific: you can target people interested in trucking, logistics companies, construction businesses, and people who work in fleet management. We've run campaigns with customer acquisition costs as low as $6-12 per qualified lead (someone who clicks through and expresses interest), though conversion rates vary 15-35% depending on offer and creative quality.

list: Create audience segments for fleet managers vs. owner-operators: These audiences respond to different messaging. Fleet managers care about uptime and relationships; owner-operators care about price and speed.

Build landing pages specific to each audience and offer: Don't send traffic to your homepage. Send fleet managers to a page about fleet maintenance plans and loyalty discounts. Send owner-operators to a page emphasizing emergency repair capability and transparent pricing.

Test 3-4 creative variations (video, carousel, single image) and run for 5-7 days: Measure which creative generates the lowest cost-per-click and scale that. We typically see video outperform static images by 25-40% in this industry.
Track phone calls and form submissions separately: Use call tracking (CallRail, Ringba) to measure leads from Facebook separately from your other channels. This helps you understand true ROI.

Retarget website visitors with offers: Once someone visits your website, follow them on Facebook with a retargeting ad offering a discount on their first service ($25 off engine diagnostic, etc.). Retargeting campaigns typically cost 50% less per lead than cold traffic.

sections.h2: The Lead Qualification and Follow-Up System

sections.p: Generating leads is step one. Converting them is everything. One truck repair shop we work with was getting 18 leads per month but only converting 3-4 into jobs. We implemented a system: (1) all leads get a text message within 1 hour with a link to book an appointment or request a quote; (2) if no response within 24 hours, an automated voice call asking them to confirm their request; (3) if still no response, an email with their estimate or next steps. This "multi-touch" approach increased their close rate from 22% to 51%. You don't need fancy CRM software—use a free tool like Calendly for appointments and Google Sheets for tracking, but the discipline matters. Respond in 1 hour, not 1 day. That 59-minute difference costs you 15-20% of your leads.

Track these metrics weekly: (1) cost per lead by channel (LSA, organic, Facebook); (2) lead-to-job conversion rate; (3) average job revenue; (4) customer acquisition cost (CAC). For truck repair, we target CAC of $30-50 per job acquired (assuming average job revenue of $200-400 and 40%+ profit margin). One shop paying $28 per lead from LSA with 48% conversion rate has a true CAC of $58, but their average job is $320 with $160 gross profit, so the math works. Another shop paying $6 per Facebook lead with 18% conversion rate has a CAC of $33, even better economics. Start with LSA (fastest results), add local SEO (best long-term ROI), and layer in Facebook Ads once you've proven your message and conversion system work. Don't spread budget across all three until each channel is optimized.

sections.quote: Truck shops that win digitally don't do everything at once. They nail one channel, measure relentlessly, then add the next. Most fail because they try Google Ads, Facebook, and 'building their brand' simultaneously and don't know which lever actually works.
